paragraphs.body: In the article, “Miffy, The Dutch Rabbit Turned Asian Hero,” Rosa Jisoo Pyo (Pyo) observes the Asian love for Miffy and defines the idea of cuteness as timeless and lifelong in Asia. Pyo explores how Miffy is created with simple lines and four main colors. She opens her article by telling us that while Miffy might be new to the West, she has been a long-time friend in Asia. Pyo supports her point that Miffy isn’t just for children by developing several important ideas. She notes that childhood doesn’t have to end and can continue into adulthood, she illustrates this by naming big brands like Mulberry and Tommy Hilfiger that have made collaborations with Miffy for consumers of all ages. She also verifies her point by sharing testimonies from Asian women and how Miffy helped them embrace their childhood. Shannen Young, who saw herself in Miffy as a child, rediscovered her connection in adulthood. Maggie Zhou reflects on being bullied for liking anime and kpop before these interests became embraced by the West. Lastly, Fabiha Yeaqub who grew up too fast and toughened too soon heals her inner child by reclaiming her soft and gentle qualities with Miffy (Pyo).

annotations.text: Pyo explores how Miffy is created with simple lines and four main colors.

annotations.comment: There are many excellent summary templates in this paragraph, but make sure your sentences and ideas are logically connected :)

MOST importantly: I wonder if your summary is "pointing" to the right ideas for your essay? I don't think your essay is about not growing up/appreciating the childlike things in life (which is where your summary points us). Your essay is about how softness/feminity/being female can be powerful, strong, and meaningful.

paragraphs.body: When I was ten years old, I was obsessed with pink, anything soft and fluffy, and adored dresses with bows and ruffles. I had always been a girly girl since I was younger, most of my clothes, toys, and even school supplies were pink. My father used to always say I was too soft and small, that I was too weak and needed to learn a strong sport. Lord knows I tried, I truly tried, but it simply hurt too much. Getting pushed around, running out of breath, and covered in sweat. I truly never could understand how anyone enjoyed it. He would smile conceitedly at me when he saw me playing tea time with my dolls. While I never understood why I was embarrassed I still felt like I was being ridiculed.

paragraphs.body: The one class that I truly disdained in middle school was the gym. A lawless land full of hyper kids that were constantly moving around. I especially hated group kickball. The group leaders would be two boys who choose their friends, then the most athletic, then the most athletic girls, and then the rotten apples of the basket, the girls who had no interest in the sport. The sighing, the long pauses, and the “com’on bro, not her” were always thrown around when it came down to choosing which team a girl should go to. It was like a humiliation ritual, seeing who would be the last to be picked, and no one wanted that. I mean I wasn’t exactly interested in winning, nor was I friends with either team leader, but I couldn’t shake this feeling of shame—

paragraphs.body: Three years later I caught “Pink-hating” sickness, where I cut my long hair, changed my wardrobe to all-black sportswear, and told everyone with a pair of ears that I hated makeup. It wasn’t like I suddenly grew a new personality, but I at least wanted to be viewed differently. I wanted to be viewed as more than just a girl, but rather someone intelligent, interesting, and strong. I wanted people to know that I wasn’t obsessed with my appearance like other girls, I was tough enough to not be messed with, and smart enough to be anything I wanted if I set my mind to it. I didn’t want to be like other girls. I didn’t want to be a girl anymore if it meant I couldn’t be seen as more than just one. Though the funny thing about being a woman is that society tells us what we are, and then gets angry when we comply. Tell us to like pink, flowers, and everything cute and soft, and then turn around to shame us for liking them. And when shaming becomes no longer tolerable and we try to become more masculine, we get ridiculed for not being womanly enough. Angry when we are feminine, and angry when we are not feminine. Like a paradox, with no real solution.

annotations.text: girl, but rather someone intelligent, interesting, and strong.
annotations.comment: Wow. The embedded message in this line is powerful. It's like there are two choices: you can either be a girl or you can be intelligent/interesting/strong.

paragraphs.body: (King) I was introduced to Miffy a few years ago through social media. I remember thinking “that's a cute bunny design” and gradually learnt of her identity that slowly took over my heart. Pyo argues that “instead of relegating a Miffy to a child’s toy, the Asian idea of cute relegates Miffy as someone to take in and take care of regardless of age.” Growing up in a family that was constantly moving, I didn’t get to keep a lot of toys and plushies as they weren’t deemed as a necessity. I only started collecting miffy items quite recently in my adult life, way past what is deemed as appropriate in some people’s eyes. But there is a precious feeling in me that is happy when I look at it. A fuzzy and warm feeling that is nostalgic to my childhood self. A source of comfort and love not only towards Miffy, but rather towards myself and child me. For the longest time I felt like I should be ashamed of liking cute things. Ashamed for liking pink, trinkets, and being a girl. I kept carrying this baggage of shame around as a badge of honor for not being like other girls. Maybe if I carried it long enough it would show that I wasn’t weak. But true strength comes when you let go of that idea. To go against what everyone else says and standing up for who you is what being tough means. To be true to yourself unapologetically and standing up proud is what true power is.

(Grove) My father never understood the appeal of collecting plushies or character stuffed toys, like Miffy. But for many Asian immigrants and Asian-Americans Miffy is much more than just a cute plush, but rather a symbol of resilience, strength, and growth. To stay tender in the face of adversity is the definition of strength. Pyo argues that Miffy is more than just a plushy and admires Yeaqub’s resilience and reports “Miffy is my way of reclaiming myself and just be okay. That it's okay to be soft. That it's okay to be gentle.” (Pyo) In making this comment, Pyo emphasizes the importance of healing your inner child and revisiting that brings you joy, even when it makes you softer and gentler. A sentiment that I heard a lot while growing up: “You are weak because you are soft.” I have heard this sentence from teachers, my parents, and male friends. Yet, I found myself the strongest when I was gentle with myself, soft in tone, and patient with my inner child. Rather than finding fault in my femininity, I have found strength in painting my nails, doing my hair, and wearing jewelry. No longer being tied to a single definition of who I am or could be. Instead, I embrace a life full of cute and fuzzy things like Miffy. Beck, Ute Kathrin. Sissi Vase. 2016. Ute kathrin Beck Keramikwerkstatt, https://utekathrinbeck.eu/en/sissi-vasen/.
